mnemonics.title: NFS Key Points
mnemonics.content: NFS: Network File System - Unix/Linux standard, stateless (v2/v3), stateful (v4), uses port 2049, mount protocol

mnemonics.title: SMB/CIFS Key Points
mnemonics.content: SMB: Server Message Block - Windows standard, uses ports 139/445, supports printing, rich ACL support

mnemonics.title: Storage Types
mnemonics.content: NAS = Files over IP (slow, simple); SAN = Blocks over FC (fast, complex)

acronyms.usage: Windows dialect of SMB for Internet sharing
Block-level storage network using Fibre Channel
Kernel abstraction layer for file systems
Permission mechanism for file access control

visualTips.visualization: Draw two diagrams: NAS shows files traveling over IP network to a NAS device with file system. SAN shows disk blocks traveling over Fibre Channel switch to storage arrays with raw disks.
Show client sends mount request → Server validates → Server exports directory → Client binds remote directory to local path → Client accesses files through local path
Draw process A requests lock → Server grants lock → Process B requests lock → Server queues request → Process A releases lock → Server grants to B

analogies.analogy: Like a library with a librarian - patrons (clients) request books (files), librarian (server) manages access, enforces rules, and maintains order.
Like a shared folder on Google Drive - you access files over internet, simple but not as fast as local storage.
Like having a direct telephone line to a warehouse - you get raw disk space, very fast but requires more technical setup.
Like keeping frequently used reference books on your desk instead of walking to the library every time - saves trips but must remember to sync changes.
